coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposite of functionalized halloysite and Ag(0) decorated magnetic carbon dots as a reusable catalyst for reduction of dyes in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Latifi Pour, Mostafa
Kazemeini, Mohammad
Sadjadi, Samahe
coredata.dc:description: A new magnetic, catalytic nanocomposite of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD was fabricated and utilized for catalyzing reductive de-colorization of methyl orange and rhodamin B in aqueous media at room temperature. To prepare this material, Cl-functionalized halloysite nanoclay was successively reacted with melamine and 2,4,6-trichloro-1,3,5-triazine to furnish multi-nitrogen containing ligand on halloysite surface. Then, magnetic carbon dots were crafted through a facile method and decorated with Ag nanoparticles and cysteine to furnish Cys-Ag/MNCD species. The final nanocomposite was obtained via incorporation of this latter species onto functionalized halloysite. This new composite was characterized through the XRD, TEM, BET, FTIR, TGA, EDX, and mapping analyses. The catalytic experiments confirmed that low loadings of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite reduction of both dyes were performed in ~1 min. This material could also be readily collected from the reaction media magnetically and displayed excellent recyclability with a slight decrease in its activity of at most ~6% up to 8 cycles. The kinetic and thermodynamic study of de-colorization reactions emphasized high catalytic performances of the synthesized nanocomposite. More precisely, activation energy, enthalpy, and entropy were determined to be 48.15 kJ mol−1, 45.65 kJ mol−1, and -124.61 J mol−1 K−1; respectively for the methyl orange while the corresponding values for the rhodamine B were found to be 11.03 kJ mol−1, 8.53 kJ mol−1, and -249.93 J mol−1. K−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical synthesis (B)
Magnetic materials (A)
Organometallic compounds(A)
Halloysite

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of Hal with CPTM: preparation of Hal-Cl
Functionalization of Hal with TCT and melamine: preparation of Hal-MT
Catalytic reduction of MO and RhB
Kinetic and thermodynamic studies of the dye reductions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the partial supports of Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute and Sharif University of Technology. Moreover, partial help of Dr. P. Mohammadi is appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic procedure for the fabrication of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD performed in this research.
a) EDX and b) The elemental mapping analyses of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite synthesized in this research.
The XRD patterns of the Cys-Ag/MNCDs and Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composites prepared in this work.
The FTIR spectra of the Cys-Ag/MNCDs and Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composites prepared in this research.
TEM images and particle size distribution of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite synthesized in the present work.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite produced in this work.
The TG curves of Hal-TCT, Cys-Ag/MNCDs, and Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD prepared in the present work.
The study of the dependency of the dye reduction reactions upon the reactor catalyst loading.
Time-dependent UV–vis spectra of MO in aqueous solution in the presence of NaBH4 using Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalyst and ln (A/A0) vs. time (inset) plot at 298K.
Time-dependent UV–vis spectra of RhB aqueous solution in the presence of NaBH4 using Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD as catalyst and ln (A/A0) vs. time (inset) plot at 303 K.

a) ln k vs
1 T
plot (Arrhenius equation) and b) ln(k/T) vs
1 T
(Eyring equation) for MO.

a) ln k vs
1 T
plot (Arrhenius equation) and b) ln(k/T) vs
1 T
(Eyring equation) for RhB.
The proposed mechanism for the reduction of MO and RhB in the presence of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD as a heterogeneous catalyst.
The results of the recycling of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD material for reductive de-colorization of MO and RhB.
Values of the reaction rate constants at different temperatures for the MO pollutant.
Values of the reaction rate constants at different temperatures for the RhB pollutant.
Thermodynamics parameters of MO and RhB reduction in the presence of the catalyst.
A chemical kinetic comparison of the catalytic performance for the reduction of MO and RhB using different magnetic catalysts being decorated with Ag particles at ambient temperature.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposite of functionalized halloysite and Ag(0) decorated magnetic carbon dots as a reusable catalyst for reduction of dyes in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gas Conversion Department, Faculty of Petrochemicals, Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gas Conversion Department, Faculty of Petrochemicals, Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new magnetic, catalytic nanocomposite of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD was fabricated and utilized for catalyzing reductive de-colorization of methyl orange and rhodamin B in aqueous media at room temperature. To prepare this material, Cl-functionalized halloysite nanoclay was successively reacted with melamine and 2,4,6-trichloro-1,3,5-triazine to furnish multi-nitrogen containing ligand on halloysite surface. Then, magnetic carbon dots were crafted through a facile method and decorated with Ag nanoparticles and cysteine to furnish Cys-Ag/MNCD species. The final nanocomposite was obtained via incorporation of this latter species onto functionalized halloysite. This new composite was characterized through the XRD, TEM, BET, FTIR, TGA, EDX, and mapping analyses. The catalytic experiments confirmed that low loadings of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite reduction of both dyes were performed in ~1 min. This material could also be readily collected from the reaction media magnetically and displayed excellent recyclability with a slight decrease in its activity of at most ~6% up to 8 cycles. The kinetic and thermodynamic study of de-colorization reactions emphasized high catalytic performances of the synthesized nanocomposite. More precisely, activation energy, enthalpy, and entropy were determined to be 48.15 kJ mol−1, 45.65 kJ mol−1, and -124.61 J mol−1 K−1; respectively for the methyl orange while the corresponding values for the rhodamine B were found to be 11.03 kJ mol−1, 8.53 kJ mol−1, and -249.93 J mol−1. K−1.

ce:section.ce:para: The most precious resource on earth is water, which is necessary for all living cells. In recent decades mainly; due to the increasing growth of industries, water pollution has become the center of attention [1]. The usage of dyes in various sectors, such as textile and paper industries, is not recent to history and dated back to more than 35000 years ago [2]. However, in modern days, the use of these hazardous chemicals has witnessed remarkable growth. In this regard, discharging vast quantities of toxic dyes such as methyl orange (MO), methyl blue (MB) and rhodamin B (RhB) into the aqueous media, not only can harm living organisms and causes environmental and biological problems but also renders the water inappropriate for irrigation, drinking and daily usages [3]. These problems have led scientists to apply methods and solutions for the de-colorization of water [4,5]. De-colorization [6], adsorption [7,8], oxidation [9], and catalytic reduction [10,11] are the most applicable methods in order to eliminate dyes from the environment.

Facile recovery and efficient recyclability of the catalysts have always been matters of concern for scientists. The use of iron oxide nanoparticles (MNPs) in the structure of the catalysts plays a significant role in simplifying the recovery of the resultant magnetic catalysts from the reaction medium [12,13]. Using MNPs in the shape of core-shells with this species at the core not only imparted magnetic characteristic to the catalyst but also provided an acceptable dispersion and limited aggregation of active species as well as; rendered an even better catalytic efficiency [14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are noble carbon nanomaterials that can be obtained from various starting raw materials such as inexpensive and green bio-wastes and graphene. Following the accidental discovery of this new member of carbon nanomaterials family at 2004 [15] and revealing their outstanding applications as time has gone by, the usage of these compounds has extended in different areas of research, namely biomedicine, catalysis, drug delivery, sensors and optoelectronics [16,17]. Remarkable properties such as significant solubility in water, chemical stability, low toxicity, and also particular surface-area-to-volume ratio, have rendered CDs as an excellent precursor for catalyst manufacturing.

Halloysite (Hal), a natural nanostructured clay with an elemental structure similar to kaolin, is in the center of attention in the past few years by virtue of its mechanical and chemical stability, availability, inertness, low toxicity, biocompatibility and high porosity [18–21]. The Hal species with the compositional formula of Al2(OH)4Si2O5(2-H2O) demonstrated a rolled shape of kaolin sheets. Hal tubes have positive charges upon their inner surface and negative charges on their outer surface, while the water molecules trapped between these surfaces [22]. Application of this clay is not limited to the catalysis and surface engineering but extended to various areas of research [23] such as drug delivery [24], nanofillers [25], anticorrosion coatings [26], adsorbent application [27] and nanoreactor for the synthesis at the nanoscale.

It is noteworthy that using the Ag nanoparticle as an active catalytic species is owed to its optical, antibacterial, and particular electrical properties [28–30]. Amongst other metals such as Au, Pd, and Pt, silver nanoparticles are considered as low-cost metals; however, due to the small sizes of these nanoparticles [31,32], their recovery may be troublesome. In this regard, fabrication of Ag nanoparticles possessing magnetic properties may provide a relief solution [29,33,34].

In pursuit of the current research on Hal based heterogeneous catalysis [35–39] and dye degradation [40,41], in the present study, a novel catalytic nanocomposite, benefiting from Hal, CD, and MNPs is devised for efficient reductive de-colorization of MO and RhB dyes under mild reaction conditions. To fabricate such a composite, initially, magnetic carbon dots, MNCDs, were prepared through the facile hydrothermal procedure and then decorated with Ag nanoparticles and cysteine to furnish Cys-Ag/MNCD species. Meanwhile, Cl-functionalized Hal was successively reacted with melamine and 2,4,6-trichloro-1,3,5-triazine (TCT) to give Hal-MT. Finally, the stabilization of Cys-Ag/MNCD on Hal-MT was carried out to form Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD nanocomposite. The activity and recyclability of the composite mentioned above were then evaluated for the reduction of MO and RhB in aqueous media at ambient temperature in the presence of NaBH4 as a reducing agent. Moreover, the kinetic and thermodynamic parameters of the reduction processes were assessed.

The chemicals used in this contribution included Hal, (3-chloropropyl) triethoxysilane (CPTM), 1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triamine(melamine), 2,4,6-trichloro-1,3,5-triazine (TCT), FeCl2.4H2O, FeCl3.6H2O, NH4.OH, urea, glycerol, AgNO3, hydrazine hydrate, cysteine, tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), NaBH4, methyl orange, rhodamine B, distilled water, and EtOH, all purchased from the Sigma-Aldrich and used as received without any further purification or pretreatment.

Using various characterization techniques such as TGA, FTIR, BET, XRD, ICP, TEM, EDX, and elemental mapping analyses, the formation of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD was verified, and its properties were investigated. The TEM images of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD were recorded by using Philips CM30300Kv field emission transmission electron microscope. ICP analyzer of Vista-pro was used to measure the silver content of the nanocomposite. Using BELSORP MINI II instrument, the textural properties of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD were studied. Prior to the analysis, the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD species was degassed by thermal treatment (heating at 150 °C for 2 h). The crystalline structure of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD was determined through the XRD technique using Siemens, D5000.Cu Kα radiation from a sealed tube in the 2θ range of 10-85°. The thermal stability of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD material was evaluated by METTLER TOLEDO apparatus under the N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. To record FTIR spectra, BRUKER, EQUINOX 55 instrument was applied. EDS analysis was performed by applying a VEGAII TESCAN scanning electron microscope. The used UV spectrophotometer for measuring and monitoring the reductive de-colorization reactions was PerkinElmer, Lambda 365.

In the first step of the catalyst preparation, the magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were prepared through a well-established co-precipitation method [42,43]. Briefly, an aqueous solution of iron salts, FeCl2.4H2O (8.0 mmol, 1.59 g) and FeCl3.6H2O (16.0 mmol, 4.32 g), was prepared and refluxed for 3 h under an inert atmosphere at 100 °C. To initiate precipitation, NH4.OH (13.0 mL, 25 wt%) was gradually added, and the resultant mixture was vigorously stirred for 2 h. Afterward, the precipitate was collected by using a magnet. MNPs were obtained after rinsing with distilled water/EtOH and drying at ambient temperature for 20 h.

The CD species was prepared according to a previously reported procedure [44,45]. In short, a solution composed of glycerol (12.5 mL) and urea (1.0 g) in distilled water (10 mL) was prepared and stirred at ambient temperature. After 10 min, the solution was transferred into an autoclave and hydrothermally treated for 4 h at 180 °C. Subsequently, the reactor was slowly cooled down, and its content was collected and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to obtain a yellow viscous CD material. In the next step, freshly synthesized MNPs (1.5 g) were added to the suspension of the CDs (10 mL) in distilled water, and the resultant mixture was then refluxed for 48 h at 80 °C. Upon completion of the reaction, the fabricated MNCDs were collected magnetically and used for the next step after washing with distilled water/EtOH and drying at 60 °C for 12 h.

For the synthesis of Ag/MNCD, a solution of AgNO3 (11.04 mg) in 40 mL distilled water was slowly introduced to the stirring mixture of MNCDs (0.5g) in 10 mL of deionized water and the resulting mixture was stirred for 2h at 50 °C. In the following step, in order to reduce the silver salt to Ag nanoparticles, 0.3 mL of fresh hydrazine hydrate was added, and the resulting mixture was vigorously stirred for 24 h at 70 °C.

To synthesize Cys-Ag/MNCDs, two mixtures were prepared. First, 1.5 mmol of cysteine was dissolved in ethanol (25 mL) to furnish solution A. Then, to prepare mixture B, 300 mg Ag/MNCD was dispersed in 25 mL of ethanol and vigorously stirred for 30 min. In the next step, solution A was added gradually to the suspension B, and the obtained mixture was stirred for 6 h at 40 °C. Finally, the product was separated magnetically, rinsed with ethanol, and dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of Hal with CPTM: preparation of Hal-Cl
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the Hal-Cl material, 1.2 g of Hal was dispersed in ample amounts of toluene with the aid of ultrasonic irradiation (power 100W for 15 min). Then, 2.5 mL of CPTM was added to the dispersed Hal, and the resulting suspension was then heated under reflux condition and inert atmosphere for 24 h at 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of Hal with TCT and melamine: preparation of Hal-MT
ce:section.ce:para: Melamine (0.05 mol) was dissolved in 75 mL DMSO and added to the suspension of 1g of Hal-Cl in 25 mL of DMSO. Next, the resulting mixture was refluxed for 14 h. At the end of the reaction, the solid (Hal-Mel) was filtered and after washing with DMSO dried in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h. In order to conjugate the TCT, 10 mmol of it was dissolved in 60 mL of the THF and then added to the suspension of 0.8 g of Hal-Mel in THF. After stirring for 2 h, the precipitate was filtered, washed with the THF, and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h.

In order to immobilize Cys-Ag/MNCD on Hal-MT, 250 mg of Cys-Ag/MNCD was suspended in 25 mL of dry toluene and vigorously stirred for 10 min. The obtained homogeneous suspension was then introduced into a stirring suspension of 125 mg of Hal-MT in 15 mL of dry toluene. Then, the resulting mixture was refluxed for 24 h at 80 °C. Upon completion of the reaction, the product was magnetically collected, withdrawn, washed with toluene, and dried. In Fig. 1 , the steps of preparation of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD are illustrated. The ICP analysis revealed that the silver content in the nanocomposite was ~1 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic reduction of MO and RhB
ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of organic dyes was performed in the following manner. The Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD and NaBH4 solution (2 mL, 0.01 M) was added to a solution of the dye (30 mg of MO or RhB in 100 mL of water), and the obtained mixture was vigorously stirred. In order to trace the progress of the de-colorization, the dye absorbance at short time intervals was measured through the UV–Vis spectrometer. To estimate the kinetic and thermodynamic parameters for this dye removal, the reduction reactions were repeated at four different temperatures, including; 25, 30, 35, and 40 °C. At the end of the reduction period, the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite was separated magnetically, repeatedly washed with EtOH: H2O (1:1 vol ratio), and dried.

The EDS analysis was performed in order to identify the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite and affirm the incorporation of the Ag/MNCD species upon the Hal-MT material. The results were displayed in Fig. 2 -a. In the EDS spectra, the peaks of silicon, aluminum, and oxygen emphasized the existence of the Hal structure. Moreover, the nitrogen and carbon peaks were ascribed to the MT functionalities upon the Hal species. The presence of iron and oxygen peaks was representative of the formation of magnetic particles. Ag peak emphasized the stabilization of the silver nanoparticles. The peaks of sulfur, oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon were all indicative of cysteine functionalized carbon dot.

Dispersion of the Cys-Ag/MNCD species was confirmed through the elemental mapping analysis and corresponding results were displayed through Fig. 2b. As depicted, the uniformly dispersed nitrogen atoms indicated that the Hal material was functionalized suitably by the MT species. Homogeneous dispersion of sulfur atoms corroborated that the cysteine had evenly covered the magnetic core-shell structure. From this analysis, it might be considered a foregone conclusion that the Ag nanoparticles were uniformly deposited upon the MNCD material. Moreover, the Fe dispersion confirmed an effective stabilization of the MNCD upon the Hal-MT species.

In order to further evaluate the structure of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite and confirm the immobilization of magnetic and silver nanoparticles, the XRD analysis was carried out and displayed in Fig. 3 . In this venue, first, the XRD pattern of the as-prepared Cys-Ag/MNCDs was determined. The observed peaks of this material were assigned as follows. The peak at 2θ = 19° (marked by black squares) was ascribed to the amorphous carbon quantum dots [46]. The sharp ones at the 2θ = 30°, 35°,43°, 54°,57°, 63° (marked by green diamonds) can be referred to as magnetic particles [33]. Also, small amounts of the Ag nanoparticles showed not high but noticeable peaks at 2θ = 38.12°, 44°, 75.3° (marked by red circles), approving the formation of Ag (0) nanoparticles [47–49]. Some of these peaks show a subtle intensity or overlapped with those of the magnetic nanoparticles. In the XRD pattern of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite, all Cys-Ag/MNCD materials were apperceived. This indicated the fact that, when being supported upon Hal-MT species, the Cys-Ag/MNCD material preserved its structure and did not collapse. On the other hand, in the XRD spectra of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite, some small peaks at 2θ = 26°, 28°, 37.1°, 40.4°, 57.5° were detected (denoted by purple triangles), which were representative peaks of the Hal material [50].

The successful synthesis of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD and Cys-Ag/MNCD composites were further inquired by the FTIR spectroscopy. The results were represented in Fig. 4 . In the case of Cys-Ag/MNCD material, the absorbance band at 3423 and 2915 cm−1 were assigned to the –OH and –CH2 groups, respectively. The bands in the range of 1581–1617 cm−1 were accredited to the –C=C and –C-O groups in the backbone of CD species. The unique band which was associated with magnetic nanoparticles occurred at 584.3 cm−1 was ascribed to the Fe-O stretching vibrations. All the discussed bands in Cys-Ag/MNCD species were apperceived in the FTIR spectrum of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD material, affirming the stability of the Cys-Ag/MNCD species in the course of functionalization. Furthermore, the sharp band at 1035.5 cm−1, as well as the bands at 3621 cm−1 and 3687 cm−1, were related to the –Si-O stretching and the internal –OH functionalities of the Hal material, respectively. The band at 1621 cm−1 was considered to be a sign of the existence of -C=N in MT structure, emphasizing proper functionalization of the Hal species.

Next, the morphological assessment of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite was undertaken. In this context, this material was subjected to a TEM analysis. The recorded images are displayed in Fig. 5 . Here, one observed a cylindrical Hal species upon which black spherical Cys-Ag/MNCDs were supported. This observation established that the Hal structure was rather stable and kept its cylindrical morphology in the course of functionalization and Cys-Ag/MNCDs immobilization. This is in good agreement with the previous results [50–52]. On the other hand, one may have inferred that the Cys-Ag/MNCD species possessed a spherical morphology, which was consistent with what was reported elsewhere [53]. Moreover, the mean diameter of Cys-Ag/MNCDs was estimated to be 13.14 ± 3 nm. Ultimately, one observed from these micrographs that the Cys-Ag/MNCDs were aggregated to some extent due to their magnetic nature.

Considering the importance of the textural properties, the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite was characterized via the BET-BJH measurements. As depicted in Fig. 6 , this material exhibited IUPAC's type II N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm. Moreover, it is well-known that the Hal species also possessed type II isotherm behavior. Thus, one may have inferred that the functionalization did not alter the N2 adsorption-desorption characterizations. Regarding the specific surface area, the BET value determined for the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite was 48.5 m2/g. This was slightly lower than the pristine Hal material being 51.0 m2/g. This decrement of the specific surface value was accredited to the placement of Cys-Ag/MNCDs onto the outer surface of the Hal species.

To establish the thermal stability of the synthesized Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite, the TG analysis was performed on the pristine Hal, Hal-MT, Cys-Ag/MNCD, and Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD materials. The resulted curves were revealed in Fig. 7 . A comparison between curves of pristine Hal and Hal-MT attested that the Hal-TCT sample displayed a weight loss at the temperature range of 240–340 °C, amounting to ~6 wt%. This implied a successful grafting of the MT species upon the Hal surface. Considering the situation for the Cys-Ag/MNCD material, a discernible weight loss of 25 wt% at 250 °C was assigned to the cysteine decomposition. In the TGA curve of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite, however, the recognized weight losses of Cys-Ag/MNCD and Hal-MT species indicated successful incorporation of such species onto one another.

In this study, a novel catalyst, Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD, was prepared for the reduction of the dyes, as mentioned above, in the presence of the NaBH4. At the outset, the essentiality of the catalyst usage and the reducing agent was assessed. In this venue, the reduction of both dyes in the absence of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite with sodium borohydride was examined. The resulting experimental data indicated that the dye concentration did not show a significant decrease. Next, the reactions were repeated using the catalytic content of Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD in the absence of any reducing agent. Similarly, no changes were observed in the dye concentration, confirming the existence necessity of both the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalyst and the NaBH4 reducing agent alongside one another to do the job. Next, the optimum loading amount of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD composite into the reacting medium was determined at the bench scale. In this regard, the progress of the reductive de-colorization of dyes at the presence of different amounts of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalyst was assessed. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the progress of the reactions was indeed dependent upon the catalyst loading. The optimum values of which for the MO and RhB were determined to be 2.0 and 3.5 mg, respectively.

Utilizing the determined optimum loading amount of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalyst, de-colorization of the MO and RhB was performed, and the progress of the reactions was followed through monitoring the amounts of the dye concentration decreases via the UV–vis spectroscopy. Results were depicted in Figs. 9 and 10
. According to these displays, the MO and RhB characteristic peaks appeared at λ = 468 and 555 nm, respectively [14,33,54]. As one may have observed, both reactions proceeded rather quickly to furnish the reduced products in quantitative terms of conversions and yields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic and thermodynamic studies of the dye reductions
ce:section.ce:para:
An inseparable part of the investigation of a catalytic performance for a newly developed material is its kinetic and thermodynamic undertakings in order to determine the mechanism of the catalytic dye reduction. In the current system, due to a large amount of reducing agent present in the reaction medium, experimental data of the reaction were fitted onto a pseudo-first-order model using the ln(At/A0) = -kt expression [55]. Here, At and A0 represented the concentrations of dye at any time t and at the beginning of the reaction, respectively. The apparent first-order rate constant of the reaction denoted by k having a dimension of sec−1 and t indicated the reaction time. As one may observe in both Figs. 9 and 10, the experimental data are in good agreement with a linear approximation of the first-order reaction. In the plot of ln(At/A0) versus t, the slope of the line leads to the value of k. The k values for each dye at four different temperatures under the same reaction conditions are provided in Tables 1 and 2
. As an obvious and expected result for each pollutant dye, the reaction rate constant was enhanced upon increasing the temperature.

Next, the activation energy of the reduction reaction of the MO and RhB was determined through the Arrhenius equation as follows:
(1)
ln
k = ln
A −
(
E a
R T
)
Where k is the reaction rate constant, A is the frequency factor, Ea is the activation energy, R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1), and T is the absolute temperature. The values of Ea for MO and RhB were calculated to form the slope of a graph of ln k versus 1/T. These were displayed in Figs. 11 and 12
. Table 3 lists the activation energy for each dye.

Thermodynamic characteristics of the reduction, such as activation enthalpy and entropy, were estimated using the Eyring equation provided below:
(2)
ln
(
k T
)
= ln
(
k B
h
)
+
Δ
S #
R
−
Δ
H #
R
(
1 T
)
Where k is the reduction rate constant, T is the absolute temperature, kB is the Boltzmann constant, h is the Planck constant, ΔS# and ΔH# are the changes in entropy and enthalpy and R are the universal gas constant. The slope and y-intercept of the linearized curve of ln(k/T) versus 1/T plot resulted in ΔH# and ΔS# values, respectively (see Figs. 11 and 12). Ultimately, the Gibbs free energy (ΔG#) was determined through the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation as follows:
(3)
Δ
G #
= Δ
H #
− T Δ
S #
The values of ΔH#, ΔS#, and ΔG# for the reduction of MO and RhB were tabulated in Table 3.

As the next part of the kinetic studies, the mass transfer limitations of the reaction were investigated. Due to vigorous agitations (i.e., of high rpm values of stirring) of solutions when removing pollutant dyes, the external mass transfer was neglected. This speed for the stirrer, which was used for all reaction runs, was obtained as a result of the conversion changes versus the agitation speed experiments. In this venue, raising the agitation speed to 800 rpm improved the de-colorization reaction. However, further increase of the stirring speed did not lead to an increment of the reaction conversion. As a result, beyond this specified speed, the external mass transfer was not limiting the reaction. Ultimately, for all dye removals, this agitation speed was utilized. On the other hand, pore diffusion as a limiting factor could play an essential role in heterogeneous reactions. In the present study, the diffusion of the dyes into the catalyst pores was examined through Weisz-Prater Criterion provided below [56]:
(4)
N
W − P
=
R
R p 2
C s
D
e f f
Where R is the reaction rate per volume of catalyst, Rp is the catalyst particle radius, Cs is the dye concentration at the particle surface, and the Deff is the effective diffusivity. Internal mass transfer can be disregarded if
N
W − P
≤ 0.3. Calculations for the present study confirmed that the Weisz-Prater criterion was
N
W − P
= 0.03. Thus the internal mass transfer limitation considerations were neglected [57,58] as well. Besides, a high rate of reaction and diminutive contact time of dye molecules also supported these findings.

An essential part of any kinetic study is the determination of the chemical mechanism involved. Although this is not an essential goal of this paper while it is an entirely different subject in its own right, here some general investigations based upon similar concepts for the MO and RhB available in the open literature [59,60] were undertaken. Hence, mechanisms for removal of such pollutants from water utilizing a newly developed Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalyst are suggested. The results were presented in Fig. 13 . Basically, the overall reaction occurred in several stages on the surface of the catalyst. First of all, detachment of NaBH4 and generation of BH4 − ions occurred in the solution. Then, borohydride ions and also the dyes, as mentioned earlier, were adsorbed on the surface of the silver nanoparticles. The formed hydride, which is produced by borohydride ions, is conveyed to the dyes, and the reduction is performed. The next stage is the desorption of the reduced dye from the catalyst. From a complementary point of view, the reduction of one mole of MO and RhB was carried out through transferring 2 and 1 electrons (or equivalently 2 and 1 protons), respectively.

Although a high activity plays a crucial role in any catalytic reaction, the catalyst recyclability should not be ignored. This latter feature of a catalyst will become very important when the process up-scaling is in order. As the next part of this study, the recyclability of the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalytic composite for the reduction of MO and RhB dyes was evaluated. Experimentally considering, upon finishing the de-colorization of MO and RhB, the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD catalyst was recovered magnetically and reused for catalyzing the de-colorization of the same dyes through their next cycle. The recovery-reuse was continued for eight runs. In order to evaluate the influence of reusing, the reaction yields of consecutive runs were compared, as illustrated in Fig. 14 . As shown, the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD material maintained its initial activity up to three runs of reductive reactions, and after that, only scant decrement of the activity of the catalyst was apperceived.

ICP analysis indicated that the silver leaching after eight reaction cycles was dropped by at most 3 wt% of its initial loading. Thus, it is a foregone conclusion that the developed catalyst is satisfyingly recyclable as well as reusable.

Chemical kinetic comparison of the catalytic performance for the MO and RhB removal between the previously published reports and those of the presently prepared materials is provided in Table 4 . This is done in order to understand better and further emphasize the performance of the synthesized catalyst in the current research. The respective reaction time is chosen as a measuring stick for this venue. It is seen that the present catalyst exhibited considerably lower time values compared to those tabulated for other magnetic catalysts decorated with Ag species reported previously and used for this purpose for both MO and RhB dye removals. These values revealed that the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD species catalyzes the reduction of MO and RhB rather rapidly hence; performs more desirably as a new comer for this purpose.

In summary, Cys-Ag/MNCD was first fabricated through a simple hydrothermal process from inexpensive raw materials and decorated with Ag nanoparticles and cysteine. Meanwhile, Hal was functionalized with melamine and TCT to provide multi-nitrogen containing ligands on its outer surface. Hybridization of Cys-Ag/MNCD and Hal-MT furnished the Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD material, which turned out to be an effective catalyst for the removal of the MO and RhB from contaminated water. The characterization of the catalyst was performed through various analyses, including; the XRD, TEM, BET, TGA, FTIR, ICP, EDX, and mapping. Delightfully, the de-colorization reactions proceeded at ambient temperature very rapidly to furnish the de-colorized dyes. Moreover, Hal-MT-Cys-Ag/MNCD material could magnetically be recovered and reused up to eight cycles. Kinetic and thermodynamic investigations of the reactions showed that activation energy, enthalpy, and entropy for the MO reduction were 48.15 kJ mol−1, 45.65 kJ mol−1, -124.61 J mol−1. K−1, respectively. For the RhB, the corresponding values were 11.03 kJ mol−1, 8.53 kJ mol−1, -249.93 J mol−1. K−1 respectively. On another issue, the internal and external mass transfer limitations were determined negligible hence not considered. Ultimately, a general chemical mechanism was suggested to pave the way for future kinetic investigations.

This statement is to declare that through submission of this paper its authors neither have any conflict of interest with any particular person, group of people or institution, nor have they submitted this paper anywhere else for publication considerations.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the partial supports of Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute and Sharif University of Technology. Moreover, partial help of Dr. P. Mohammadi is appreciated.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Podolean, O.D. Pavel, H.G. Manyar, S.F.R. Taylor, K. Ralphs, P. Goodrich, V.I. Parvulescu, C. Hardacre, SCILLs as selective catalysts for the oxidation of aromatic alcohols, Catal. Today, 333 (2019) 140-146.
